title: Chapter Nine: The Lake
content: I can't stay inside.

The walls are wrong this morning. They've been wrong for days, but today they pulse — or maybe that's me, my heartbeat pressing outward against my skull until the edges of every room seem to throb in time. The cameras don't help. I've started cataloguing them: the brass fixture in the east corridor, the recessed lens above the staircase, the black eye in the kitchen ceiling that swiveled toward me when I reached for the kettle. Maybe it didn't. Maybe it always pointed that way. Maybe I'm the one who moved.

I put on my coat. I don't tell anyone I'm leaving. There's no one to tell — Flora hasn't emerged from her rooms, and Miles has been absent since breakfast, a ghost himself, drifting through doorways before I can catch his eye. The house accepts my departure without comment. The front door opens. The step down onto the gravel is cold through my soles. I walk.

The path to the lake is shorter than I remember. Or maybe I'm walking faster. The grounds of Bly Manor slope downward through a tatter of winter-killed gardens, past the greenhouse with its shattered panes, past the stone fountain that hasn't held water in decades. The gravel turns to grass turns to mud. I don't look back at the house. If I look back, I'll see the windows, and the windows are eyes, and the eyes are watching.
The lake unfolds before me like a wound in the earth.

Wide shot: gray sky pressed flat against black water, no horizon between them, just a seam where the clouds dissolve into their own reflections. The willows stand at the margins like mourners, their trailing branches combing the surface. No birds. No wind. The silence is so complete it has texture — a weight settling against my eardrums, pressing inward until I can hear my own blood.
I breathe. The air tastes like iron and wet stone.

This is better. Not safe — I don't think safe exists anymore — but safer. Out here, there are no walls. No cameras mounted in corners with their patient red lights. Just the sky and the water and the mud squelching under my boots as I walk the perimeter. I follow the curve of the shore, keeping the lake on my left, the slope of the manor's grounds on my right. The grass gives way to reeds, and the reeds give way to a strip of dark silt, littered with debris — twigs, dead leaves, a bottle, a child's shoe rotted to laces and sole.
And something else.

It catches the light. Or rather, it almost doesn't — a glint swallowed by the mud, half-buried at the waterline where the lake has receded and left its trash behind. I stop. Crouch. My knees sink into the cold muck and I don't care. My fingers close around the object and pull.
A ring.

Silver, thin, delicate. A woman's ring. The band is tarnished but intact, and the setting holds a chip of dark stone — onyx, maybe, or just glass. Mud clings to the engraving inside. I rub at it with my thumb. The cold metal bites my skin. I bring it closer, squinting, and read:
My pulse ticks. Then ticks again, faster.
Rebecca Jessel.

The ring Peter Quint's ghost-lover would wear. The ring a woman lost while fleeing across these grounds in the dead of night, dragging herself toward the water or away from it, the gravel biting her bare feet, the manuscript she was carrying already ruined by rain. The ring the official record never mentioned because the official record never mentioned her at all.

I know this because I looked her up. Three nights ago, hunched over my laptop at 2 AM, the glow of the screen the only light in my room, I read every cached forum post about the Bly Manor case. The vanished governess. The missing valet. The children who stopped speaking for eleven days and then, when they started again, spoke as if nothing had happened. And at the center of it all: Rebecca Jessel, who walked out of this house and into the lake and never came back.
Or did she?
The ring is small in my palm. Cold. Real. I close my fist around it and feel the press of the band against my skin like a whisper.

I need to wash it. The mud is thick, obscuring the detail, and I need to see — I need to *know* — whether this is what I think it is. I move to the water's edge. The lake lies still and black before me. I kneel. The mud soaks through my jeans. I reach down and dip the ring into the water.
The surface goes glassy.
Not still — glassy. The small ripples I made with my hand flatten and vanish as if they were never there. The lake becomes a mirror, and in that mirror—
A woman. Standing behind me.

Close-up: dark hair falling past her shoulders. Dark dress, high-collared, antique. Her face is angled down, toward the top of my head, as if she's looking at me. As if she's been looking at me for a long time. Her expression is neutral — not angry, not sad — just *present*, the way a person stands when they're waiting for you to notice them.
She is standing directly behind me. In the reflection. In the water.
My breath stops.

I don't move. I can't move. The lake holds the image with perfect clarity, more vivid than the gray day around it, more real than the cold seeping through my knees. The woman doesn't move either. She stands with her hands at her sides and her face tilted down toward me and she is *right there*, close enough to touch, close enough that if I turned—
I turn.
Nothing.

Willows. Gray sky. The manor on the hill, its windows catching what thin light there is. The empty shore stretching in both directions. The absolute silence of a world that has not, at any point in the last several seconds, contained another human being.
I turn back to the water.
The figure is still there.
She's smiling.

I don't think. I don't breathe. My hand fumbles in my coat pocket and finds my phone, and I hold it out in front of me with both hands like a shield, and I take the photo. The shutter sound cracks the silence — a mechanical, stupid little click, the kind of sound that belongs to the world where reflections behave and the dead stay drowned. I look at the screen. The image is there, captured: the black water, the mirror surface, and in it, the woman. Dark hair. Dark dress. Smiling.

I open Reddit. My fingers are shaking so badly I mistype my password twice. I post the photo to r/Glitch_in_the_Matrix — raw, unedited, no filters, no crops, nothing. I type the title with my thumbs moving faster than my thoughts:
*I found this at the lake. What am I looking at?*
I hit post. The little wheel spins. The post goes live.
Then I stand there, in the mud, at the edge of the black water, and I wait.
The comments come in waves.

The first wave is predictable. GET OUT NOW. Leave that place. OP are you INSANE. This is a demon/ghost/apparition/viral marketing campaign and you need to run. Exclamation points. All-caps. The kind of fear that reads as excitement, the kind of adrenaline that tastes like entertainment through a screen.

The second wave is worse. Fake. Obvious Photoshop. Nice try OP. The reflection doesn't even match the lighting. This is clearly staged. Someone found a Victorian dress and stood behind their friend. Boring. 2/10.

I scroll through them standing in the cold, my fingers numb on the phone. The ring sits in my pocket, a small hard weight. The lake is just a lake again — wind-stippled, ordinary, reflecting nothing but the sky. The woman is gone.
The third wave is different.

It comes from a commenter called @SkepticalSherlock. Their avatar is a magnifying glass. Their post history is a litany of debunked ghost photos, exposed fakes, frame-by-frame analyses of "paranormal" footage that turned out to be lens flare or pareidolia or deliberate manipulation. They are, by their own description, "the wet blanket this sub deserves."
Their comment is long. I read it three times.

*"Okay, I went through the EXIF data and the metadata checks out — no editing software, no timestamps that don't match. Assuming OP didn't go full forensic on us, this photo is unmanipulated. That said, let's talk about what we're actually seeing.*
*I ran the image through enhancement to check the reflection figure's proportions. Here's what I found:*

*The jawline matches OP's. I mean it MATCHES — same angle of the mandible, same chin width, same ratio of lower face to mid-face. The hair color and texture are consistent with OP's (based on previous photos in her post history). The height differential between the reflection figure and what we can see of OP's own reflected silhouette is zero — they're the same height, or close enough that the margin of error is negligible.*
*That's not a ghost, OP. That's YOU.*

*But here's where it gets interesting. The angle is wrong. If you're crouching at the water's edge and looking down at your reflection, you should see your own face from below — chin up, nostrils, the underside of your brow. The face in the water is looking DOWN at you. From above. From an angle that would require you to be standing directly over yourself.*
*Either this photo is manipulated in a way I can't detect — and I'm pretty good at detecting — or something very wrong is happening to your sense of self.*
*Not trying to scare you. Just calling it like I see it."*
I close my eyes. Open them. Read it again.
The jawline matches. The hair matches. The proportions match. It's my face. My face, looking down at me from an angle that shouldn't exist, smiling with an expression I have never made in my life.
I look up at the manor.
The east window. Second floor. Miles stands there, framed in the glass, very still. He is looking down at me at the lake. His hands are at his sides. His face is expressionless.
Like he's seen this before.
Like he knows how it ends.

I hold his gaze for three seconds. Four. He doesn't move. He doesn't wave. He doesn't come to the window or call down or do any of the things a person does when they notice someone standing at the edge of a lake in the freezing cold, staring at their phone. He just watches. And then he turns away from the glass and is gone, swallowed by the dark interior of the house, as if the window was never anything but a screen.
I look back at the water.

My reflection stares up at me. Just my reflection — pale face, dark circles under the eyes, hair wind-tangled, mouth slightly open. The lake is ordinary again. The woman is gone. There is no second figure, no impossible angle, no smile that doesn't belong to me.
But @SkepticalSherlock is right.

I pull up the photo. Zoom in on the face in the water. The jawline. The jawline is mine. The proportions are mine. But the angle — the angle is from above, from behind, from a vantage point I could never occupy unless I was floating six feet over my own body.

And the smile. That smile. I've never smiled like that. I don't think I could. It's the smile of someone who knows something you don't, someone who's been watching you struggle with a puzzle they solved years ago, someone who finds your confusion — what's the word — *amusing*.
My face. My face, smiling at me from a place I cannot reach.

The ring is cold in my pocket. Rebecca Jessel's ring. R.J. A woman who walked into this lake and didn't come back. A woman who, if the stories are true, came back anyway — just not in her own body, not on her own terms, not in any way the people who loved her could recognize.

I put the phone away. I stand in the mud at the edge of the black water, and the gray sky presses down on me, and the willows trail their fingers in the lake, and somewhere behind me, in a house full of cameras and children who do not act like children, Miles Ashford watches from windows and waits.

The reflection in the water is mine. Just mine. Just me, pale and frightened and rapidly becoming someone I don't recognize. But the photo on my phone tells a different story. The photo says: there is a version of you that exists outside your control. A version that moves when you don't move, that smiles when you don't smile, that watches from angles that should be impossible.
A version of you that has been here before.

The wind picks up. The lake surface shivers and breaks, dissolving the reflection into a thousand scattered fragments. I watch them ripple and settle. Watch my face come apart and reassemble, come apart and reassemble, like a puzzle that keeps solving itself in new and unsettling arrangements.
I whisper it to the water, to the sky, to the watching window where no one stands anymore:
"Who am I when I'm not looking?"
The lake does not answer. But I think — I *think* — that something underneath the surface smiled.
